data.title: U.S. crude stockpiles fell 4.4M barrels last week, API says
data.description:
The American Petroleum Institute reportedly shows a larger-than-expected draw of 4.4M barrels of oil in U.S. commercial stockpiles for the week ending April 17, after increasing by 6.1M barrels in the previous week.

Gasoline inventories reportedly show a draw of 5.16M barrels for the week, distillate inventories reportedly show a draw of 4.59M barrels, and inventories at the Cushing storage hub reportedly show a build of 678K barrels.

The Energy Information Administration is scheduled to release its weekly U.S. petroleum supply report on Wednesday; the average of analysts surveyed by The Wall Street Journal forecast domestic commercial crude stocks would decline by 1M barrels, gasoline inventories were expected to drop by 1.4M barrels, and distillate inventories were expected to decline by 1.5M barrels.

WTI front-month June crude oil (CL1:COM) recently traded at $90.19/bbl, up from Tuesday's $92.13/bbl settlement price.

ETF: (USO)

data.title: USAID shutdown fallout ripples through Washington economy
data.description:
The dismantling of the U.S. Agency for International Development last year has evolved from a political flashpoint into a broader economic story, exposing how deeply federal agencies and their contractor ecosystems are woven into the Washington region’s labor market and consumer economy, the New York Times reported Monday.

For investors, the episode highlights the economic leverage of government payrolls and procurement spending, particularly in regions heavily dependent on public-sector activity. Sharp reductions in federal employment can weigh on housing demand, local banks, consumer spending and service-sector businesses.

Companies tied to consulting, outsourcing, healthcare administration, education and real estate in the Washington metro area may face softer demand when federal dollars retreat. The situation also underscores how policy shifts can quickly reshape employment trends and regional growth prospects.

Nearly all of USAID’s roughly 16,000 employees were dismissed, while hundreds of thousands of contractors, nonprofit partners and overseas hires tied to agency programs also lost work. One year later, many former employees remain unemployed or underemployed, according to interviews and informal surveys cited by former staffers, the Times reported.

The effects extend beyond individual job losses. Cuts to USAID coincided with wider reductions in federal staffing, grants and contract spending, helping push Washington, D.C., to the highest unemployment rate in the nation at 6.7%, the report said. Former workers described drawing down savings, tapping retirement accounts, moving in with relatives and relying on public assistance while navigating a weak job market.

For supporters of the cuts, the USAID overhaul represented a long-overdue crackdown on what they viewed as a bloated bureaucracy that funneled taxpayer money into inefficient programs and ideologically driven causes overseas.

Many affected workers came from specialized fields such as international development, healthcare administration, logistics and foreign assistance. Several said they had submitted dozens of job applications with limited success, suggesting that skills built in federally funded systems don’t always transfer smoothly into the private sector. Others accepted lower-paying work or part-time roles while searching for permanent positions.

Beyond economics, the shutdown also raises questions about the long-term value of U.S. soft power and international influence. Former staff members argued that dismantling an agency representing less than 1% of the federal budget may carry geopolitical costs disproportionate to the savings achieved.

Some former employees have found new opportunities in local government, academia, entrepreneurship and nonprofit work. Still, the broader picture is one of a disrupted professional network and a cautionary example of how sudden policy decisions can cascade through labor markets, local economies and investor sentiment.

Dear Readers: We recognize that politics often intersect with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Grupo Aeroméxico, S.A.B. de C.V. reports Q1 results

data.title: National Healthcare Properties prices $462M stock offering
data.description:
• National Healthcare Properties (NHPAP) on Tuesday said it has priced a public offering of 38.5 million Class A common shares at $12 each.
  • The offering is expected to raise about $462 million in gross proceeds, with shares set to begin trading on the Nasdaq Global Market on April 22 under the symbol “NHP.”
  • Closing is expected on April 23, subject to customary conditions.
  • The company has granted underwriters a 30-day option to purchase up to an additional 5.78 million shares to cover overallotments.
  • National Healthcare Properties said it plans to use proceeds to repay about $186 million of debt under its revolving credit facility, fund potential acquisitions and for general corporate purposes.
  • NHPAP -0.04% after hours to $21.22.
  • Source: Press Release

data.title: Merck gains approval of HIV drug regimen Idvynso
data.description:
• The US FDA has approved Merck's Idvynso, a two-drug single-pill regimen of Pifeltro (doravirine) and islatravir for HIV.
  • Doravirine is a non-nucleoside reverse transcriptase inhibitor, while islatravir is considered a novel nucleoside reverse transcriptase translocation inhibitor. The latter stops HIV-1 replication by inhibiting reverse transcriptase translocation and inducing structural changes in viral DNA.
  • Approval was based on the results of two randomized, non-inferior trials. In the double-blind study, patients were switched from Gilead Sciences' (GILD) Biktarvy (bictegravir/emtricitabine/tenofovir alafenamide) to Idvynso.

data.title: Driven Brands expects Q4 revenue between $450M and $460M, receives Nasdaq non-compliance notice
data.description:
• Driven Brands (DRVN) announced on Tuesday that Q4 2025 revenue is expected at $450M–$460M, FY 2025 at $1.85B–$1.86B, and Q1 2026 at $475M–$485M.
  • The company expects adjusted EBITDA of $100M–$110M in Q4 2025, while FY 2025 EBITDA is expected to decline YoY due to one-time costs from financial restatement work.
  • The company had about $130 million in cash and cash equivalents as of March 28, 2026.
  • Consensus for Q4 revenue is $458.61M.
  • Net debt improved to ~$1.6B, down from about $2.1B at the end of 2025.
  • The company also received a Nasdaq compliance notice on April 15, 2026, due to delayed 2025 Form 10-K and Q1 2026 Form 10-Q filings; despite this, the stock remains listed.
  • It has been given a 60-day window (until June 15, 2026) to submit a compliance plan and up to October 12, 2026, to regain full compliance.
  • The company said it is working to complete the review and will limit communications until the restatement process is completed.

data.title: Gold, silver post sharp slide as dollar, yields climb
data.description:
Gold futures fell for the sixth session out of the past eight, pressured by a rally in the dollar and a rise in U.S. Treasury yields, while investors awaited any sign of progress in the U.S.-Iran conflict and Federal Reserve Chair nominee Kevin Warsh's confirmation hearing.

"Stronger yields and the dollar are putting pressure on gold, along with a ​lot of headlines and mixed signals coming out about the Iran situation, causing energies to move higher, putting pressure on metals," RJO Futures senior market strategist Bob Haberkorn said in a note.

President Trump said he did not want to extend the expiring ceasefire on Iran and ​that the U.S. military was ready if negotiations failed, which drove crude oil prices up more than ‌3%; after-hours, Trump said the U.S. will extend its ceasefire with Iran while continuing with its naval blockade of the country's ports until its leaders present "a unified proposal."

Prices for gold and silver look vulnerable to another tumble, Forex.com analyst Razan Hilal said in a note, pointing out that both precious metals are moving similarly to how they moved earlier this year: a positive trend before giving way to a quick collapse.

Starting in early March, gold futures went from $5,300/oz to a low of below $4,400/oz by the end of the month, and silver saw similar albeit wider movements; both metals have since rebounded but may be exposed to another large reversal, Hilal said.

Front-month Comex gold (XAUUSD:CUR) for April delivery fell 2.2% to a two-week low  $4,698.40/oz, and front-month Comex April silver (XAGUSD:CUR) tumbled 4.43% to $76.411/oz, its lowest settlement value since April 13.

ETFs: (GLD), (GDX), (GDXJ), (IAU), (NUGT), (PHYS), (GLDM), (AAAU), (SGOL), (DUST), (RING), (BAR), (OUNZ), (SLV), (PSLV), (SIVR), (SIL), (SILJ)

data.title: RBB signals NIM moving toward 3.25% while prioritizing sub debt retirement in 2026
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: RBB Bancorp (RBB) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "The first quarter was a strong start to the year with continued earnings growth, expanding margin and further improvement in our credit metrics," said (CEO, President & Director Johnny Lee), citing net income of $11.3 million and $0.66 per share, ROA of 1.09%, and net interest margin (NIM) of 3.15% as "our fifth consecutive quarter of margin expansion." Lee also said loan growth was modest (up about $11 million) as $131 million of originations at a 6.4% average yield were offset by "elevated payoffs and pay-downs," while adding, "Our pipelines remain healthy, and we continue to believe we are positioned to deliver stronger loan growth over the balance of the year."

  • "Net income for the first quarter was $11.3 million or $0.66 per diluted share," said (Executive VP & CFO Lynn Hopkins), adding net interest income rose $1 million to $30.5 million as interest expense fell $1.4 million and interest income declined $390,000. Hopkins attributed the funding improvement to "lower rates on retail deposits" following "the Federal Reserve rate cuts made towards the end of 2025," and highlighted mix shift: "Retail deposits increased by $50 million and included a shift from time deposits into a high-yield savings product."

  • "Nonperforming loans remained basically unchanged," Hopkins said, while noting "we had effectively no net charge-offs" and "recorded a small reversal of provisions for credit losses." (Executive VP & Chief Credit Officer Jeffrey Yeh) added, "NPL actually this is virtually not changed during the quarter," and described the quarter as "pretty quiet" with one relationship exiting and one entering.

Outlook

  • No validated analysts-estimates comparison was included because the provided estimates data did not follow the transcript-period labeling conventions required for validation.

  • On profitability drivers, Hopkins said the FHLB special dividend was not recurring: "the FHLB special dividend is onetime in nature." On the medium-term NIM trajectory, she added, "we still have an opportunity to expand our margin from a more normalized basis, which is probably closer to or just above 3%" and that management believes it has "the opportunity" to reach "3%, 3.25%" over time, tied to deposit mix and C&I growth.

  • On loan growth expectations, Hopkins framed 2026 as "mid- to high-single digits," while Lee echoed that the bank is "looking into high single digits" but emphasized it would remain "very disciplined" on "quality" and "appropriate returns" when competing on pricing.

  • On expenses, Hopkins guided, "We expect noninterest expense for the next few quarters to be in the $18 million to $19 million range."

Financial Results

  • Hopkins reported net interest income of $30.5 million and NIM of 3.15% (up from 2.99% in Q4), adding that the quarter’s NIM benefited from the FHLB dividend: "The FHLB dividend added 4 basis points to our NIM in the first quarter." She also said funding improved as "an 8 basis point decline in the overall cost of funds" contributed to the NIM increase.

  • Noninterest income rose to $4.3 million, which Hopkins said was driven by "$890,000 higher net gain on REO," "$484,000 in a recovery on a fully charged-off acquired loan" and "$360,000 of interest income on tax refunds related to purchased federal tax credits." Noninterest expense increased to $19.3 million, but Hopkins said "our efficiency ratio improved to 55% from 59% in the fourth quarter."

  • On balance sheet and capital metrics, Hopkins reported total assets of $4.2 billion, loans held for investment up $11 million since year-end, deposits down $10.5 million, book value per share of $31.10, and tangible book value per share up 2% to $26.84, while emphasizing the deposit mix shift: "we reduced wholesale funding and grew lower costing retail deposits."

Q&A

  • Brendan Nosal, Hovde Group: Asked about large nonperforming assets and normalized reserve levels; (CFO Hopkins) responded, "90% of our NPLs are represented by the same three relationships" and said coverage "has an opportunity to move lower," while (Chief Credit Officer Yeh) said NPLs were "virtually not changed" and one exit/one entry occurred; Hopkins added, "our largest one is working its way through a bankruptcy process" and "we do see an opportunity for NPLs to be resolved during 2026."

  • Nosal, Hovde Group: Asked about capital deployment and buybacks; (CFO Hopkins) said sub debt is the near-term priority: "I do think the sub debt is our first priority" and "there may be a good reason to look to retire a good portion of it," noting it "does require regulatory approval."

  • Nosal, Hovde Group: Asked if the FHLB dividend benefit was one-time and about margin path; (CFO Hopkins) said "the FHLB special dividend is onetime in nature" and flagged sub debt repricing and quarter-length effects, adding NIM could be pressured by normalization but still has room to improve as deposits reprice.

  • Kelly Motta, KBW: Pressed on muted loan growth vs. strong pipelines; (CEO Lee) cited seasonality and pricing discipline, saying the bank avoided competing at "submarket rates" and "did sort of let go...of a few deals," while adding, "pipeline is still very healthy" and he was "very positive about the pipeline overall."

  • Motta, KBW: Asked about further deposit-cost reductions and CD roll dynamics; (CFO Hopkins) said "there is still a little bit of opportunity" given the quarter-end spot rate and described deposit repricing, including that about "1/3" of certain balances can reprice and that competitive offers are "as high as 4%."

  • Motta, KBW: Asked about potential citizenship-data requirements; (CEO Lee) said the bank was mainly monitoring SBA-related guidance and concluded, "We don't have any -- there's no impact based upon our system."

  • Matthew Clark, Piper Sandler: Asked if high single-digit loan growth is still achievable; (CFO Hopkins) pointed to seasonal strength in Q2/Q3 and anchored expectations at "mid- to high single digits," while (CEO Lee) reiterated discipline on "quality" and "appropriate returns."

  • Clark, Piper Sandler: Requested specifics on CD maturities and promotional savings pricing; (CFO Hopkins) said "about 60% of our deposits sit into the CDs and sort of Flex savings products" and cited market pricing for rate-sensitive deposits of "between 3.85% and 4%."

  • Jackson Laurent, Stephens: Asked about returning to a 4% NIM; (CFO Hopkins) tied that history to deposit mix, saying a 4%-handle NIM would require "a very high percent of noninterest-bearing deposits" and that management is focused on "building the commercial or C&I part of our business" before discussing that level.

  • Laurent, Stephens: Asked about fee outlook and loan sales vs. retention; (CFO Hopkins) described mortgage loan sales as "higher volume, lower premiums" and SBA sales as "smaller dollar volume but higher premiums," adding SBA premiums should be "similar, if not higher levels than what we were able to achieve in 2025."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative to neutral in tone, emphasizing shortfalls and sensitivities (loan growth slower than expected, margin sustainability, deposit pricing pressure, and capital actions), including Motta’s framing that loan growth was "more muted than we had expected" and Laurent’s question about a path back to a "4% NIM."

  • Management tone was slightly positive and operationally confident, leaning on momentum in NIM and credit, with Lee calling Q1 "a strong start" and Hopkins stating "we still have an opportunity" for margin expansion, while also qualifying items as non-recurring ("onetime") and dependent on market/regulatory factors.

  • Versus Q4 2025, management in Q1 2026 used more definitive near-term performance language ("strong start," "highest quarterly earnings level in 2 years"), while Q4 2025 emphasized work-in-progress on credit ("we still have work to do") and described deposit competition as "intense"; analyst questioning remained centered on the same core areas (margin, deposits, loan growth, credit), with Q1 adding more detail requests on repricing mechanics and longer-term margin ceilings.

Quarter-over-quarter Comparison

  • Profitability and margin discussion shifted from gradual improvement in Q4 (NIM cited at 2.99%) to a clearer step-up in Q1 (NIM at 3.15%), while management simultaneously flagged one-time support ("FHLB special dividend") and normalization effects that could pressure reported margin.

  • Loan-growth narrative moved from Q4 optimism for "another year of high single-digit growth in 2026" to a more bounded framing in Q1 of "mid- to high-single digits," with greater emphasis on turning away lower-priced competition and acknowledging elevated payoffs.

  • Capital deployment emphasis became more sequenced in Q1: management reiterated that sub debt action comes first, with buybacks presented as an additional opportunity after addressing the sub debt repricing and capital treatment changes.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management highlighted that problem assets remain concentrated and not yet fully resolved, with Hopkins noting, "we're elevated on our NPLs" and the largest credit "working its way through a bankruptcy process," while still indicating a potential resolution timeline: "opportunity for NPLs to be resolved during 2026."

  • Pricing competition and funding costs were described as active constraints on growth and margin upside, with Lee noting competitors offering "submarket rates" and Hopkins observing some banks offering deposits "as high as 4%," while mitigation centered on discipline, deposit repricing, and continued shift away from wholesale funding.

Final Takeaway

Management characterized Q1 2026 as a step forward in earnings and margin expansion, driven by lower funding costs, improving deposit mix, and stable-to-improving credit. Leadership reiterated that loan growth is expected to re-accelerate later in 2026 but tied that outlook to maintaining underwriting and pricing discipline amid competitive loan and deposit markets. In capital planning, management prioritized addressing the repricing sub debt—potentially retiring a significant portion—while keeping buybacks as a subsequent lever, alongside continued focus on resolving a small set of concentrated nonperforming relationships during 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript
